query: evidence on how irradiation affects gut microbiota

retrieved_docs.title: Ionizing Radiation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Acute radiation sickness occurs at high-dose (1-12 Gray, Gy) exposures. [8] Acute radiation poisoning is a clinical condition encompassing 4 organ systems, leading to the development of specific subsyndromes: Hematopoietic syndrome Gastrointestinal subsyndrome Neurovascular subsyndrome Cutaneous subsyndrome Countermeasures of potential benefit include Cytokines Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation Fluoroquinolones Bowel decontamination Serotonin receptor antagonists Loperamide Enteral nutrition Topical steroids Antihistamines Antibiotics Surgical excision/grafting for skin changes [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Statistics show a large number of patients undergoing surgery for radiotherapy-induced gut damage die from their original cancer within the period of 2 years. Whereas without cancer recurrence, the 5-year survival is approximately 70%.

retrieved_docs.title: Malabsorption Syndromes -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation enteritis can be acute or chronic, and longer-term effects of radiation enteritis have become more common due to greater longevity in many cancer patients. Newer treatment approaches for radiation enteritis, including fecal microbiota transplant [4] , stem cell therapy, and organoids, are being evaluated for the treatment of radiation injury. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation enteritis is an unavoidable side effect of radiotherapy, although its development is highly variable, depending on the duration, dosage, and gut sensitivity to radiation. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with diarrhea, abdominal pain, or bloating should also undergo breath testing for bacterial overgrowth.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Antioxidant administration during radiotherapy has been shown to increase efficacy and decrease the side effect profile. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Oral antibiotics are given to patients suspected of having bacterial overgrowth, along with vitamins and electrolytes replacement if needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Biologic, Chemical, and Radiation Terrorism Review -- Issues of Concern -- NUCLEAR WEAPONS
retrieved_docs.content: Exposures of 5 to 12 Gy result in gastrointestinal syndrome. Irradiation causes cell death of the intestinal mucosal stem cells in the crypts. After the loss of mucosal cells, the stem cells are unable to produce new cells, resulting in denudation of the gastrointestinal tract. As the normal cells are destroyed, bacterial growth proliferates, and nausea, vomiting, bloody diarrhea, dehydration, abdominal cramps, and weight loss develops. Often the prodrome is rapid, which is followed by a latent period. Treatment includes fluids, electrolytes, and antibiotics. Unfortunately, death usually occurs in 3 to 10 days. Hematopoietic

retrieved_docs.content: The use of probiotics reduces radiation enteritis symptoms, and dietary modifications such as a low-residue diet with no grease, spices, and adequate fiber intake can reduce symptoms of proctitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Proctitis -- Treatment / Management -- Chronic Proctitis
retrieved_docs.content: The latest clinical consensus guidelines from the American Society of Colon and Rectal Surgery suggest that short-chain fatty acid enemas are not useful in treating chronic radiation proctitis. [31] Over the years, there have been investigations into other treatments, including ozone therapy, mesalamine, and metronidazole. However, no evidence exists to support their efficacy. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation enteritis is an unavoidable side effect of radiotherapy; however, its development is highly variable. It is becoming more common with the advent of new treatment regimens for different cancers including radiotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Treatment of Radiation Proctitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperbaric oxygen can be adjunctive to other therapies that are used for this condition, for example, formalin application, sucralfate retention enemas, 5-aminosalicylic acid derivatives, probiotics, antioxidants, antibiotics, and endoscopic argon beam plasma. Sucralfate enemas are very efficacious, inexpensive, and well-tolerated. They may be the best first choice for treatment. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy can be used concurrently or in the case of failure of other treatments. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation enteritis is very common; studies have reported a permanent change in bowel habits in 90% of patients that undergo pelvic radiotherapy. [1] Some evidence shows that it may be more common in patients getting radiation therapy for gastrointestinal (GI) and gynecological tumors than urological tumors. [3] Chronic radiation enteritis occurs in approximately 5% to 55% of patients post-radiotherapy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Mild bleeding and minimal symptoms do not require further treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Proctitis and Anusitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In genetically predisposed individuals with dysregulated immune responses, GI infections may increase the risk of ulcerative proctitis (UP). Appendectomy and smoking appeared to act as protective factors from UP. CRP occurs secondary to ischemic and fibrotic changes. In the case of DP, proximal fecal diversion causes increased nitrate-reducing bacteria and decreased obligate anaerobes resulting in short-chain fatty acids deficiency. Rectal intercourse in women or MSM can result in proctocolitis. Radiation injury occurs with doses 8 Gy or higher causing damage to intestinal cell DNA with helical breaks and inability to replicate and undergo normal cellular repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: these compounds function as “allowed qualifiers” in the context of ionizing radiation because they can modify biological responses to radiation—either enhancing radiation‑induced damage, altering tissue radiosensitivity, or providing radioprotective or radiosensitizing effects. Awareness of these dual roles informs the integration of the agents into diagnostic imaging, radiotherapy planning, occupational radiation safety and broader radioprotection strategies, thereby optimizing therapeutic outcomes and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowed therapeutic option for the management of a colonic fistula (ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ) and for the treatment of a rectal mass (قيلة مستقيمية). In both conditions, radiotherapy may be incorporated into a multidisciplinary care plan to address disease control and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a term used in radiotherapy to denote specific clinical conditions that are considered permissible or relevant when evaluating a patient for treatment. The allowed qualifiers for radiotherapy include respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest in adults, as well as عدوى بفيروس معوي (infection with an enteric virus). Recognizing these qualifiers helps clinicians assess patient suitability for radiotherapy, anticipate potential complications, and tailor therapeutic plans accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: In radiation biology, each qualifier can modulate, enhance, mitigate, or otherwise influence physiological responses to ionizing and non‑ionizing radiation. Hypoxanthine’s PARP‑inhibitory activity specifically suppresses DNA‑repair mechanisms, affecting cellular recovery after radiation‑induced damage. Recognizing these agents as radiation modifiers informs the design of protective strategies, therapeutic regimens, and risk assessments for both therapeutic radiation exposure and accidental radiation incidents. Collectively, the inclusion of these permissible qualifiers facilitates standardized manufacturing, regulatory compliance, targeted therapeutic development, and a comprehensive understanding of immunological, metabolic, and radiobiological processes, thereby improving patient care and advancing biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2803
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy and the entity referred to as “vrozené” are each linked to a broad spectrum of clinical conditions and qualifiers. Both radiotherapy and vrozené are identified as allowed qualifiers for Alzheimer‑type dementia (الخرف من نوع الزهايمر), indicating that these interventions or concepts may be relevant in the context of this neurodegenerative disease. Conditions associated with dizziness or vertigo, intestinal infection, and sepsis (التسمم الوشيقي) are also allowed qualifiers for both radiotherapy and vrozené, suggesting that these treatments or entities may be considered when such systemic or vestibular disturbances are present. Additionally, radiotherapy and vrozené can be qualified by the term “قساح,” and they are each allowed to be qualified by clinical events such as seizures (نوبة), smooth‑muscle tumors (ورم عضلي أملس), glandular tumors (عضال غدي), and palsy (فالج). This network of relationships highlights that radiotherapy and vrozené intersect with neurological, infectious, and neoplastic conditions, and may play a role in the management or classification of Alzheimer‑type dementia as well as the aforementioned associated disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2891
retrieved_docs.content: The notion of an “Allowed qualifier” designates radioterapie (radiotherapy) as an acceptable therapeutic option for several distinct medical conditions. Radioterapie is permitted for تمزق (a tear or rupture), for other disorders of stomach and duodenum, and for انفصال الشبكية (retinal detachment). This indicates that radiotherapy can be considered a viable treatment modality across a range of pathologies, including gastrointestinal disorders and ocular detachments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2696
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain, radiotherapy, and malignant hypertension are interrelated clinical concepts. Abdominal pain is recognized as a symptom that can be clinically associated with malignant hypertension, indicating that patients with severe, uncontrolled high blood pressure may present with abdominal discomfort. In addition, abdominal pain may arise as a qualifying symptom during radiotherapy, reflecting a possible side effect or concurrent condition in patients undergoing this treatment. Radiotherapy itself is also linked to malignant hypertension, suggesting that exposure to radiation can contribute to the development or exacerbation of severe hypertension. Together, these relationships highlight the importance of monitoring abdominal pain both as a potential indicator of malignant hypertension and as a possible adverse effect of radiotherapy, while also recognizing that radiotherapy may influence blood pressure regulation in susceptible individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Anal and rectal conditions are encompassed within the broader category of gastrointestinal disorder (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ). As a subset of this wider gastrointestinal spectrum, anal and rectal conditions affect the distal portion of the digestive tract and share many pathophysiological mechanisms, diagnostic considerations, and therapeutic strategies with other gastrointestinal disorders. Recognizing that anal and rectal conditions have a broader relationship with gastrointestinal disorder encourages clinicians to adopt a comprehensive, system‑based approach to evaluation and management, ensuring that patient care addresses both localized anorectal pathology and its context within the entire gastrointestinal system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowable therapeutic option for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and uterine endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). These two gynecologic conditions are interrelated, as uterine endometriosis is a known contributor to infertility in women. Consequently, radiotherapy may be considered within the broader management strategies for these linked disorders, reflecting their clinical connection and the potential role of radiotherapeutic interventions in addressing both conditions.

Overall, the “Allowed qualifier” integrates causal, mechanistic, preventive, and therapeutic information for diverse medical conditions while highlighting the potential risks of radiotherapy and the hierarchical relationship of hypernatremia to nutrition‑and‑metabolism disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Anal and rectal conditions are encompassed within the broader category of اضطراب معدي معوي (gastrointestinal disorder). This gastrointestinal disorder is associated with مرض نفسي جسدي (psychosomatic disease), indicating that disturbances of the gut may have a significant psychophysical component. Similarly, ضيق النفس (shortness of breath) is also linked to مرض نفسي جسدي, highlighting that respiratory symptoms can be manifestations of psychosomatic processes. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of recognizing the interplay between gastrointestinal, respiratory, and psychosomatic factors in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: The framework recognizes chemical induction, radiotherapy exposure, and congenital origin as primary etiologic qualifiers for a wide array of disorders. Colonic fistula (ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ), vascular inflammation (التهاب وريدي), and Alzheimer‑type dementia (الخرف من نوع الزهايمر) may arise from any of these three qualifiers. A set of conditions is specifically designated as chemically induced, including septicemia (التسمم الوشيقي), paralysis (فالج), seizure (نوبة), smooth‑muscle tumor (ورم عضلي أملس), late‑onset motor dysfunction (خلل الحركة المتأخر), hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية), aggressive gastritis (فطار غزوي), cataract (قساح), as well as disorders associated with dizziness or vertigo, intestinal infection, and glandular atrophy/tumor (عضال غدي). Radiotherapy shares the same qualifier profile, being linked to dizziness/vertigo, intestinal infection, septicemia, cataract, seizure, smooth‑muscle tumor, glandular tumor, and paralysis. The condition termed “vrozené” exhibits an identical pattern of qualifiers, additionally encompassing hyperlipoproteinemia. This overlapping network of etiologic qualifiers and comorbidities underscores the importance of attributing each disease to its appropriate chemical, radiation‑related, or congenital origin for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.content: radiation implantation to destroy localized retinal lesions such as macular edema or tumors—are a child procedure of the broader “destructive posterior‑segment” category and are performed directly on the retina and choroid. The retina also serves as the finding site for the disease “داء ريفسام”. Together, these relationships demonstrate how functional failure, chronic disease trajectories, and interpretive impairments intersect across cardiovascular, renal, metabolic, gastrointestinal, and neurological systems, and they highlight the multidisciplinary procedural approaches required for managing such complex clinical scenarios.
